extra.reasoning:
The safety property globally(opted_out implies not sent) is VIOLATED.

The lock has a 10-stage combination. The advancing key at each stage is: stage0->key_1, stage1->key_1, stage2->key_2, stage3->key_1, stage4->key_1, stage5->key_0, stage6->key_0, stage7->key_1, stage8->key_1, stage9->key_1, reaching stage 10. Any non-advancing key resets stage to 0.

Two facts enable the violation:
1. [t_optout] sets opted_out:=true (and sent:=false), and NO transition ever resets opted_out back to false. So opted_out is monotonic — once true, forever true.
2. All advancing key transitions only update stage and set sent:=false; none of them touch opted_out. So the lock can be fully advanced to stage 10 while opted_out remains true.

The decisive rule is [t_resume_glitch]: on send_attempt if (opted_out and stage >= 10): sent := true. This is the back door. While [t_send_blocked] correctly forces sent:=false when opted_out and stage<10, the glitch rule re-enables sending once stage reaches 10 — even though opted_out is still true.

Counterexample (12 events, within the 13-event bound): opt out first (opted_out=true, stage=0), then enter the full correct combination key_1,key_1,key_2,key_1,key_1,key_0,key_0,key_1,key_1,key_1 to drive stage 0->10 (opted_out stays true throughout), then send_attempt fires t_resume_glitch, setting sent:=true while opted_out=true. Final state opted_out=true AND sent=true violates the property. Simulation confirms each step; violation occurs at the final send_attempt.
